summary: Fowler argues that remote versus co-located work isn't a simple binary choice, but rather a spectrum of distribution patterns including single-site, multi-site, satellite workers, and remote-first teams. While he believes most people are more productive when co-located due to richer communication, he contends that remote teams can often be more productive overall because they can draw from a wider talent pool. The key is understanding the trade-offs of each pattern and investing in communication accordingly. He concludes that organizations able to make remote working effective will have growing competitive advantage as top developers increasingly resist relocation and commuting demands.
key_points: There are multiple distribution patterns (single-site, multi-site, satellite, remote-first), each with distinct trade-offs and techniques
Most individuals are more productive co-located, but remote teams can be more productive overall by accessing better talent
Satellite workers are the hardest pattern to make effective—they tend to become increasingly detached from the main team
Multi-site teams should divide work by autonomous components, not by layer or activity, due to Conway's Law
Remote-first requires going all-out—all communication online, regular in-person meetups, and caution with junior staff
money_quotes: A remote team may be less productive than that same team if it were co-located, but may still be more productive than the best co-located team you can form.
The key point is the point where you find it easier to send an email than walk over to talk.
Such a rule means you aren't able to hire the best person for the job, you can merely hire the best person who is prepared to relocate.
I've heard a few people argue that agile software development is incompatible with remote working. That's poppycock.
It's very difficult to get satellite workers to be effective. With most people co-located, most communication will happen within the co-located team.
I sense a growing reluctance amongst the best developers to accept the location and commuting disadvantages of single-site work.
key_insight: The productivity question isn't whether remote or co-located is better in isolation, but whether the talent advantages of remote work outweigh the communication advantages of co-location for your specific situation.
